title: Statement of Cash Flows
description: Premium Analysis Guide: AS vs Ind AS vs IFRS vs US GAAP

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION A: Core Technical Comparison Table
tableData: Provide information about historical changes in cash.
Provide information about historical changes in cash and cash equivalents.
Provide information about historical changes in cash and cash equivalents.
Provide information about cash receipts and cash payments.
Exemptions exist for SMCs and certain non-corporate entities.
Mandatory for all entities preparing financial statements under Ind AS.
Consistent core structure across all frameworks.
Short-term, highly liquid investments convertible to known cash.
Short-term, highly liquid investments readily convertible to known cash.
Short-term, highly liquid investments readily convertible to known cash.
Similar, but explicit rules on restricted cash.
Classified as financing liabilities. Not part of cash equivalents.
Included in cash equivalents if repayable on demand and form an integral part of cash management.
Included in cash equivalents if repayable on demand and form an integral part of cash management.
Excluded from cash equivalents. Treated as financing activities.
Significant difference between IFRS/Ind AS and US GAAP/AS 3.
Judgment required; generally excluded from cash equivalents if restricted.
Judgment required; generally excluded from cash equivalents if restricted.
Must be included in the total cash balance shown in the cash flow statement; reconciled to balance sheet.
US GAAP is prescriptive; IFRS/Ind AS require judgment.
Operating activities - Direct vs Indirect method
Direct method explicitly encouraged, but Indirect permitted.
Direct method encouraged, Indirect permitted (if Direct used, reconciliation to net income required).
Indirect method is the practical standard globally.
US GAAP is strict on using Net Income.
Major comparability issue across standards and peers.
US GAAP includes all interest in operating.
US GAAP treats as operating return on investment.
Operating activity unless specifically identifiable with investing/financing.
Operating activity unless specifically identifiable with investing/financing.
US GAAP bans allocation to investing/financing.
Classified according to nature and separately disclosed.
Operating lease = Operating activity; Finance lease = Financing (principal) & Operating (interest).
US GAAP operating leases remain in operating cash flows.
Recorded at exchange rate on transaction date.
Recorded at exchange rate on transaction date; unrealized gains/losses separately presented.
Recorded at exchange rate on transaction date; unrealized gains/losses separately presented.
Changes in ownership interests in subsidiaries (no loss of control)
Aligned. Transactions with owners in capacity as owners.
Include only cash flows between investor and investee (e.g., dividends).
Include only cash flows between investor and investee (e.g., dividends).
Include only cash flows between investor and investee (e.g., dividends).
Consistent focus on actual cash movements.
Generally Investing (if related to original transaction/liability).
Generally Investing (if related to original transaction/liability).
Operating (if soon after acquisition - <= 3 months) or Financing (later).
US GAAP is prescriptive and different.
Disclosure of changes in financing liabilities
Explicitly required (roll-forward of debt/lease liabilities, separating cash and non-cash changes).
Not explicitly required as a single roll-forward, but expected via other disclosures.
IFRS/Ind AS requirement adds significant transparency.
Classified in same category as the cash flows of the hedged item.
Classified in same category as the cash flows of the hedged item.
Generally same as Ind AS/IFRS, but some complex exceptions.

Interest & Dividends (Non-Financial) Interest paid: Financing activity (non-financial enterprise) Interest received: Investing activity (non-financial enterprise) Dividends paid: Financing activity Dividends received: Investing activity Taxes Paid Operating activity (separate disclosure) Extraordinary Items Required separate classification Bank Overdrafts Not included in cash equivalents Indirect Method Start Point Net profit/loss

Interest & Dividends (Non-Financial) Interest paid: Operating OR Financing (policy choice) Interest received: Operating OR Investing (policy choice) Dividends paid: Financing OR Operating (policy choice) Dividends received: Operating OR Investing (policy choice) Taxes Paid Operating unless specifically identifiable with investing/financing Extraordinary Items Not applicable (prohibited) Bank Overdrafts & Disclosures Included if criteria met Changes in financing liabilities: Required disclosure

Interest & Dividends (Non-Financial) Same as Ind AS 7 (fully converged). Provides choices for interest and dividends classification. Bank Overdrafts Bank overdrafts integral to cash management included in cash equivalents. Extraordinary Items Extraordinary items prohibited.

Interest & Dividends (Non-Financial) Interest paid: Operating activity (no choice) Interest received: Operating activity (no choice) Dividends paid: Financing activity (no choice) Dividends received: Operating activity (no choice) Taxes Paid Always operating (no allocation) Bank Overdrafts & Disclosures Bank overdraft: Not included (financing activity) Restricted cash: Included in total cash but separately disclosed Indirect Start Point Net income only

          Classification)

            impact)
tableData: Varies based on policy choices for interest/dividends
US GAAP excludes interest received from CFI

SECTION F: Balance Sheet vs Cash Flow Statement Presentation (Year
            1)
tableData: Ind AS/IFRS reduces opening cash by overdraft amount
Derived after applying net cash flows to opening C&CE

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION G: Cash Flow Impact - Reconciliation

tableData: Varies heavily depending on interest/dividend classification
Economic FCF unchanged, but standard FCF varies by label
Can appear distorted by operating vs financing choices
Normalize by using Total Net Cash Flow for pure comparability
Significant shifts depending on if dividends drop CFO or CFF
Interest reduces numerator in some regimes, denominator in others
Overdraft netted in CCE for Ind AS vs separated in others

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION I: Earnings Quality - Signals Table
tableData: Non-cash charges or working capital sources exceed uses
Quality of earnings may be high if working capital changes are sustainable; but one-time working capital benefits may reverse
Analyze sustainability of working capital changes
Aggressive revenue recognition, build-up of receivables, or high non-cash charges
Red flag - earnings not converting to cash; potential quality issues
Interest classified as operating (Ind AS/IAS 7 policy choice)
Company elected to show higher operating cash flow by excluding interest from CFO (if classified as financing)
Presentation choice - not economic; creates artificially higher CFO compared to peers with different policy
Dividends classified as operating (Ind AS/IAS 7 policy choice)
Rare - may be used by entities wanting to show lower financing outflows
Unusual policy - check if appropriate for business model; may mislead on dividend sustainability
Bank overdraft included in cash equivalents (Ind AS/IAS 7)
Company has right of offset and overdraft is integral to cash management
Legitimate under IFRS/Ind AS but reduces reported net debt artificially
Restricted cash included in total cash (US GAAP)
Legal or contractual restrictions exist but cash is still reported in cash flow statement
Not available for general use - liquidity may be overstated
Adjust available cash by excluding restricted portion
Disclosure of changes in financing liabilities (IFRS/Ind AS only)
Company provides roll-forward of debt, leases, and other financing obligations
Good governance - allows tracking of non-cash changes (e.g., new leases, conversions)
Use this disclosure to understand true financing cash needs
Classification-driven level shift (if interest/dividend classification changes)
Persistent as long as policy remains
Calculate cash flow from operations before interest and taxes by adding back interest and taxes to CFO
Ind AS company with interest in financing has higher CFO than IFRS peer with interest in operating - normalize
Use consistent definition across periods: FCF = CFO (standardized) - Capex
Calculate unrestricted cash available for operations by deducting overdrafts and restricted cash
Ind AS shows higher cash balance due to overdraft
Level shift (if overdraft netted against cash)
Calculate gross debt separately from cash
Ind AS net debt lower due to cash overdraft netting
Use cash flow before interest and taxes / interest paid for all periods
Company with interest in financing shows differing coverage - same economics
Higher under US GAAP; varies under IFRS/Ind AS
Recast all to common basis: CFO before interest and taxes
Economic cash generation from operations is identical; only labels differ
Calculate cash flow before interest and taxes / EBIT for apples-to-apples
True cash conversion quality independent of financing classification
Calculate total net cash flow from all activities - non-operating investing
Economic FCF is identical - ignore classification differences
Appears stronger if interest in financing (CFO not reduced)
Use (CFO + interest paid)/Total Debt or use EBITDA/Total Debt
Coverage unaffected by classification; use pre-interest cash flow
Appears lower under Ind AS (overdraft in cash)
Add back overdrafts to net debt; exclude restricted cash
Leverage higher than reported - adjust for netting
Why does operating cash flow differ between two similar companies under IFRS?
Under IAS 7/Ind AS 7, companies can choose to classify interest as operating OR financing, and dividends as operating OR financing
Two identical companies can show different CFO solely due to policy choice
Should I include bank overdrafts in cash when analyzing liquidity?
Under IFRS/Ind AS, overdrafts included if repayable on demand AND integral to cash management; under US GAAP, never included
For cross-border comparability, exclude overdrafts from cash for all companies
How do I compare US GAAP and IFRS cash flow statements?
Normalize to common classification - move all interest and dividends to operating for US GAAP companies; for IFRS, reclassify interest and dividends to match US GAAP or use pre-interest CF
Prepare bridge table: IFRS CFO + interest in financing = comparable to US GAAP CFO
Why does Ind AS 7 encourage direct method but no one uses it?
Direct method requires tracking gross cash receipts/payments - more costly; most ERP systems not configured to provide this data easily
Most Indian companies use indirect method despite encouragement
How do I treat restricted cash in analysis?
US GAAP includes restricted cash in total cash in cash flow statement but reconciles to balance sheet; IFRS requires judgment
Exclude restricted cash from available-for-use liquidity metrics
What's the most important disclosure in Ind AS 7 that AS 3 lacked?
Changes in financing liabilities - shows both cash and non-cash changes to debt, leases, and other obligations
Critical for understanding true financing cash needs and non-cash financing activities
Compare CFO across companies without understanding classification differences
May draw incorrect conclusions about relative performance
Focus on total cash flow, not operating classification; adjust for working capital sustainability
Reclassify all cash flows to consistent basis before comparing; ignore presentation differences
Understand classification differences; request policy election disclosures
Use standardized cash flow before interest and taxes for peer comparisons; value based on total FCF
CFO coverage ratios for covenant testing
Most impacted - classification differences can artificially inflate or deflate coverage ratios
Write covenants based on cash flow available for debt service = CFO + interest + taxes - maintenance Capex
Focus on total cash flow from all activities; less concerned with classification
Use simple cash balance change; operating classification irrelevant for cash runway analysis
CFO higher than if classified as operating; may be trying to show stronger operating performance
Reclassify interest to operating for apples-to-apples with US GAAP peers
CFO lower but more comparable to US GAAP; may be trying to show realistic operating cash flow
No adjustment needed for US GAAP comparison
Dividends classified as operating (IFRS/Ind AS - rare)
Inflates financing cash flow (less negative) by hiding dividend outflows in operating; may mislead on dividend sustainability
Always reclassify dividends to financing for analysis
May be trying to manage reported CFO trends; requires disclosure and retrospective restatement
Restate prior periods to same policy; question management motivation
No disclosure of changes in financing liabilities (IFRS/Ind AS)
Hiding non-cash financing activities (e.g., new leases, debt conversions)
Request disclosure; adjust analysis to include non-cash financing
Provides transparent gross cash receipts/payments; most transparent presentation
Highly positive signal - use direct method data for more accurate cash flow forecasting

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION P: Red Flags vs Green Flags (Visual Indicator Matrix)
tableData: Consistently huge gap between Operating Cash Flow and Net Income
Check for aggressive working capital management or aggressive revenue recognition.
CFO positive only because of delayed payments to suppliers (payable stretch)
Unsustainable. Recompute CFO assuming normal payable days.
Capitalizing routine operating expenses into Investing Cash Flow
Manually move these outflows back to CFO to find true cash generation.
CFO covers Capex and Dividends comfortably over a 3-year cycle
Strong indicator of a self-sustaining business model.
Clear breakdown of cash flows by segment or geographical region
Use granularity to model segment-specific terminal growth rates.
